data.title: Should Apple buy Intel's stock? These analysts suggest a better investment.
data.description:
By Emily Bary

Intel reportedly approached Apple about an investment, but absent 'obvious benefits' to the smartphone giant, some analysts say the company would be better off just repurchasing more of its own stock

Apple, led by Tim Cook, could spend upwards of $90 billion on buybacks this fiscal year. Bernstein analysts say those are a better use of cash than a hypothetical stake in Intel.

Intel Corp. (INTC) reportedly has had early-stage talks about a potential investment from Apple Inc. But analysts at Bernstein see a better use for that hypothetical cash.

"Without obvious benefits to Apple ... we would far prefer the company to buy back its own shares vs buying [Intel] shares," Bernstein analysts said in a Thursday note to clients.

Apple (AAPL) could spend $92 billion on buybacks this fiscal year, according to FactSet estimates, after having spent $95 billion last fiscal year. The company boosts its buyback authorization annually at levels that are the highest in the market. Based on data from Birinyi Associates from late August, which looked at all U.S. companies, Apple occupied the top six spots for annual buyback announcements and shared the No. 7 spot.

Companies buy back their shares as a means of returning capital to shareholders and lowering the share count, which in turn can boost earnings per share. Buybacks can also suggest to investors that executives see a company's stock as undervalued at current levels.

Read: Will Apple deliver Intel its next lifeline? Here's why analysts think a deal would make sense.

It's also worth noting that Nvidia Corp. (NVDA) agreed to invest $5 billion in Intel last week, though that amount pales in comparison to the latest $60 billion boost to Nvidia's buyback authorization that was announced in August.

See also: Nvidia has $57 billion in cash. Wall Street has ideas about what to do with it.

After Bloomberg News reported late Wednesday that Intel reached out to Apple about a possible investment as well as a closer relationship, the Bernstein team acknowledged that a stake was at least "plausible." Apple "is large, has plenty of cash, and (in theory) could use alternative sources of leading-edge manufacturing capacity." Intel, meanwhile, has been lining up investors - first with the U.S. government and then with Nvidia Corp.

But money doesn't seem to be the biggest issue for Intel, according to the Bernstein team. Intel isn't "in any imminent cash crunch" and rather needs better technological innovation to win over customers who can fill the manufacturing capacity it'll likely need to boost to satisfy the Trump administration.

Intel has struggled with technological missteps over the years and even if the government tries to induce U.S. companies to use Intel's chip-fabrication capabilities, the Bernstein team struggles to see how the company could attract "meaningful volume" without dramatically improving its capabilities.

"Thus we continue to wonder why companies have been so eager to throw money Intel's way given their track record; we suspect another sort of encouragement is likely happening here," the analysts said. They later noted that an investment could provide "possible further ingratiation with the current U.S. administration," which admittedly "in itself could make this deal worth it if it continues to avoid worst case scenarios for tariffs."

-Emily Bary

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-25-25 1323ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: KalVista Pharmaceuticals Shares Drop After Notes Offering Prices
data.description:
By Chris Wack 




  KalVista Pharmaceuticals shares were 9% lower, at $11.83, after the company priced its offering of $125 million of 3.25% convertible senior notes due 2031 in a private placement. 



  The company said the offering was increased from the previously announced offering size of $110 million. 



  KalVista also granted the initial purchasers of the notes an option to buy, for settlement within a 13-day period from, and including, the date on which the notes are first issued, up to an additional $18.75 million of notes. 



  The sale of the notes is expected to close Monday. 



  The notes will be senior, unsecured obligations of KalVista, and will bear interest at a rate of 3.25% per year, payable semi-annually in arrears on April 1 and Oct. 1 of each year, beginning on Oct. 1, 2026. The notes will mature on Oct. 1, 2031, unless earlier converted, repurchased or redeemed in accordance with the terms of the notes. 



  KalVista estimates the net proceeds from the offering will be $120.8 million, or $139 million if the initial purchasers exercise their option to purchase additional notes in full. 



  KalVista expects to use the proceeds from the offering for working capital and other general corporate purposes. 





  Write to Chris Wack at chris.wack@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  September 25, 2025 13:27 ET (17:27 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Hippocratic AI and ERAAS Health Partner to Deliver Real-Time, Climate-Responsive Outreach for At-Risk Populations
data.description:
Hippocratic AI and ERAAS Health Partner to Deliver Real-Time, Climate-Responsive Outreach for At-Risk Populations

Hippocratic AI today announced a strategic partnership with ERAAS Health Inc., a provider of precision population health solutions at the intersection of weather and health. Together, the two companies have already demonstrated the power of their collaboration through the launch of a first-of-its-kind initiative that uses real-time climate intelligence and generative AI to protect vulnerable New Yorkers during adverse weather events. As historic heatwaves swept through the region and triggered a state of emergency, the program proactively delivered thousands of personalized alerts to at-risk members, offering actionable safety tips, directions to nearby cooling centers, and seamless connections to care management and community resources.

At the heart of this initiative is ERAAS Health’s cutting-edge platform, which monitors environmental conditions and identifies individuals at elevated health risk using advanced analytics, data science, and Hippocratic AI’s generative AI healthcare agents. By translating climate signals into clinical insights, ERAAS Health empowers health plans and state agencies to act before weather-related crises lead to preventable hospitalizations. With a mission to save lives and reduce healthcare costs, ERAAS Health is bringing together technologists, clinicians, and public health experts to solve an urgent human challenge of keeping populations safe in an increasingly volatile climate. From heatwaves to wildfires, poor air quality, pollen surges, and extreme cold, ERAAS Health enables a year-round climate-health response.

These insights are operationalized at scale through Hippocratic AI’s Polaris Constellation System, a patented platform that delivers safe, empathetic, multilingual outreach via generative AI healthcare agents. These agents conduct real-time conversations with members, offering not just information but meaningful engagement. When a need is identified, whether it's transportation assistance, a social determinant barrier, or a complex clinical concern, the system closes the loop with care teams or local resources.

Results from the most recent heat wave underscore the effectiveness of this model. Thousands of outreach calls were conducted. 10% of completed calls resulted in member follow-ups, demonstrating both high relevance and utility. Additionally, the member experience has been overwhelmingly positive, with calls earning an average satisfaction rating of 9.02 out of 10, a testament to the safety, empathy, and trustworthiness of Hippocratic AI’s conversational agents.

“Proactive outreach can save lives. Through our partnership with ERAAS Health, we’re operationalizing climate data into compassionate, real-time conversations that help people stay safe and connected to care,” said Munjal Shah, CEO of Hippocratic AI.

“Our secure platform ingests clinical data, marrying it with environmental data for all seasons, to identify vulnerable members and provides the orchestration layer for proactive personalized member engagement. This integrated system with Hippocratic AI can be activated in less than a day and is a game-changer today for Payors and Providers to protect their members from sudden and foreseen environmental risks,” said Samrat Kulkarni, Founder and CEO of ERAAS Health Inc.

By combining climate intelligence with proactive AI-driven outreach, the Hippocratic AI and ERAAS solution prioritizes the member experience and safety. The outreach is a model of responsive outreach that provides support and information to vulnerable populations at the right time.

About ERAAS Health Inc.

ERAAS Health Inc. is focused on the intersection of weather and health, leveraging advanced analytics, data science and generative AI technology to prevent unnecessary hospitalizations and save lives. The company’s platform ingests granular weather and environmental data to create an actionable intelligence-layer highly customized for healthcare use-cases such as Care Management and Precision Population Health. The founding team consists of board certified clinicians, weather experts and technologists from Fortune 10 companies.

About Hippocratic AI

Hippocratic AI has developed a safety-focused Large Language Model (LLM) for healthcare. The company believes that a safe LLM can dramatically improve healthcare accessibility and health outcomes in the world by bringing deep healthcare expertise to every human. No other technology has the potential to have this level of global impact on health. The company was co-founded by CEO Munjal Shah, alongside a group of physicians, hospital administrators, healthcare professionals, and artificial intelligence researchers from El Camino Health, Johns Hopkins, Stanford, Microsoft, Google, and NVIDIA. Hippocratic AI has received a total of $278 million in funding and is backed by leading investors, including Andreessen Horowitz, General Catalyst, Kleiner Perkins, NVIDIA’s NVentures, Premji Invest, SV Angel, and six health systems. For more information on Hippocratic AI, visit www.hippocraticai.com.

Media Contact:
Rick Keating
press@hippocraticai.com

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20250925305955/en/

data.title: Wall Street is starting to rethink the need for multiple rate cuts into 2026
data.description:
By Vivien Lou Chen

Traders pare back rate-cut expectations into early next year after Thursday's data showed surprising strength in the U.S. economy

Financial-market participants are scaling back slightly on their expectations for as many as five to seven interest-rate cuts by October 2026 following unexpectedly strong U.S. data on Thursday.

The U.S. economy is looking stronger than many people previously thought, which is giving way to a reconsideration by traders of how low interest rates might need to go into next year.

The rethink is occurring in a subtle way after Thursday's data showed fewer-than-expected initial jobless claims and a surprising upward revision in second-quarter economic growth. The market-implied likelihood of a quarter-point rate cut by the Federal Reserve in October slipped to 85.5%, from 91.9% a day ago, according to the CME FedWatch Tool. But expectations for a similar-size move by December were also slipping, along with the chances of additional easing into next year.

Read: Now that the Fed has cut rates, investors can focus on what really matters for markets

For now, the economy is signaling "more strength, less need for rate cuts," said economists Lindsey Piegza and Lauren Henderson at Stifel, Nicolaus & Co. in Chicago. In a note, they wrote that "an even stronger growth profile in Q2, led by additional strength in consumption and investment, reiterates the storyline of a solid economy despite fiscal-policy uncertainty, relatively elevated price pressures and a reduced pace of hiring."

The impact of this surprising economic strength showed up on Thursday in the form of selling in the bond market, sending yields higher on everything from the 1-month Treasury bill BX:TMUBMUSD01M through the 30-year bond BX:TMUBMUSD30Y. The benchmark 10-year Treasury rate BX:TMUBMUSD10Y rose as much as 5.5 basis points to an intraday high of almost 4.2%, after breaking through the key support level of 4.15%.

Thursday's climb in yields appeared to reflect reduced concerns about the possibility that a softening labor market might translate into broader economic weakness and could require the Fed to keep cutting interest rates. Meanwhile, stock-market investors, who have been hopeful about getting multiple rate cuts without a recession, were sending all three major U.S. stock indexes DJIA SPX COMP to their first joint three-day losing streak in months.

"Our view is that there are two types of consumers, creating two different sides to this market," said portfolio manager Brian Mulberry of Chicago-based Zacks Investment Management.

"There are those who owned homes during the pandemic, refinanced their mortgages at 2.5% to 3%, paid off their loans and are not feeling the price pressure of inflation. Consumers who didn't own a home or other assets during this five-year period of time are really struggling, and that is showing through as a bifurcated economy," Mulberry said in a phone interview. "We can make the case that asset owners are still driving growth in the economy. But if they turn negative, start saving and stop spending, we could see a risk of slowing growth or stagflation."

Last week, Fed officials cut rates by a quarter of a percentage point, to between 4% and 4.25%, to address downside risks to employment, while signaling that two more moves of the same size would likely occur by December, followed by a third reduction in 2026. By comparison, fed-funds futures traders have been expecting as many as five, and maybe even six or seven, quarter-point rate cuts by next October - even after scaling back their expectations on Thursday.

Thursday's selling of U.S. government debt was sending the policy-sensitive 2-year yield BX:TMUBMUSD02Y up by around 5.5 basis points to 3.65%. The 30-year rate BX:TMUBMUSD30Y rose less than 1 basis point to almost 4.77%.

-Vivien Lou Chen

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-25-25 1315ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: PSQ Holdings Stock Slides on Plans to Launch Crypto Treasury Service
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan 




  Shares of PSQ Holdings declined after the company said it plans to launch a cryptocurrency treasury as a service for its clients. 



  The stock fell 22% to $2.21 on Thursday. Shares are down about 52% this year. 



  The financial technology company said Thursday it will use IDX Advisors' crypto technology to allow businesses across its ecosystem to integrate cryptocurrencies and stablecoins into their operations. 



  "In short, our merchants gain the ability to act as their own banks while capitalizing on the rapid growth of crypto finance," the company said. 



  The move aims to position the company at the intersection of technology and distribution, helping it to monetize every transaction and service amid as the economy shifts away from traditional finance toward cryptocurrency, Chief Executive Michael Seifert said. 





  Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  September 25, 2025 12:56 ET (16:56 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: James S. Farrin Receives "Triangle Business Journal" 2025 'Corporate Leadership Award'*
data.description:
James S. Farrin Receives "Triangle Business Journal" 2025 'Corporate Leadership Award'*

James S. Farrin Receives "Triangle Business Journal" 2025 'Corporate Leadership Award'*

PR Newswire

DURHAM, N.C., Sept. 25, 2025

Founder and President of the Law Offices of James Scott Farrin is recognized on award list for invaluable contributions to the Triangle community.

DURHAM, N.C., Sept. 25, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- The Law Offices of James Scott Farrin is proud to announce that its Founder and President, James S. Farrin, has been honored with a "Triangle Business Journal" 2025 'Corporate Leadership Award – Role Model.'*

This award recognizes C-level executives who embody integrity, vision, and community impact while guiding their organizations to lasting success.** "Corporate leadership is not just about dollars and cents. In today's world, leaders are expected to inspire, motivate and guide their communities as part of being a good corporate citizen," said Sougata Mukherjee, market president and publisher of "Triangle Business Journal."

Farrin was selected for his decades of leadership in building one of North Carolina's largest injury law firms and for his ongoing dedication to social responsibility and access to justice. Earlier in the year, the Law Offices of James Scott Farrin was named to the "Best Lawyers" 2025 'Best Law Firms' list – its 10th consecutive year being honored.* 

"Leadership is about more than running a business—it's about serving people and setting an example," said Farrin. "From our clients and staff to the broader community, I've always believed that putting people first is the foundation of meaningful success. I am deeply honored to receive this recognition."

The "Triangle Business Journal" celebrated this year's award recipients at a ceremony on September 18th, bringing together figures across industries who stand for leadership and positive influence in the Triangle region.

ABOUT THE LAW OFFICES OF JAMES SCOTT FARRIN
The Law Offices of James Scott Farrin is one of the largest personal injury firms in North Carolina and has helped over 73,000 injured people since 1997. Operating from 14 offices in NC and one in SC, many of the firm's 60+ accomplished attorneys are recognized professionals in their fields. The firm focuses on providing quality legal services to as many people as possible in the following practice areas: personal injury, car accidents, workers' compensation, whistleblowing, defective products, eminent domain, mass torts, class actions, and Social Security Disability.

Contact Information:
David Chamberlin
555 S. Mangum Street
Suite 800
Durham, NC 27701
1-866-900-7078
https://www.farrin.com
Offices in Durham (main), Raleigh, Charlotte, Greensboro, NC, and Greenville, SC.
Attorney Michael Shepherd: 555 S. Mangum Street, Suite 800, Durham, NC

*For standards of inclusion, visit bizjournals.com. For 'Best Law Firms' standards of inclusion, visit bestlawyers.com.

**Prior results do not guarantee similar outcomes in future cases because each case is unique and must be evaluated separately.

data.title: The Fed doesn't think inflation will run amok again. The PCE price gauge will help clue us in.
data.description:
By Jeffry Bartash

Inflation seen as peaking around 3% due to tariffs, then subsiding again

People shop at a supermarket in Montebello, Calif. Inflation has not returned to low prepandemic levels.

The Federal Reserve's preferred inflation barometer won't show a very worrisome increase on Friday, but investors want to know if there's any devil in the details.

The personal-consumption expenditures (PCE) price index is forecast to show a 0.3% rise in August, but a smaller 0.2% advance in the more closely followed core measure. The core rate strips out volatile food and energy costs and is a better predictor of future inflation.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell previewed the PCE report at a public event on Tuesday.

Calculations by the central bank's economists, he said, signaled the main index would show a 2.7% increase in the 12 months ended in August, up from 2.6% in the prior month.

The core rate would show a one-year increase of 2.9%, unchanged from the prior month.

Both of those Fed forecasts match up with the monthly Wall Street forecasts.

To be sure, inflation is running above the the Fed's long-run goal of 2% inflation. Yet Powell and other senior Fed leaders are relieved inflation hasn't risen as much as expected due to higher U.S. tariffs.

The Fed delayed any reductions in interest rates this year until it could get a better idea of how much the tariffs were contributing to inflation. So far, the effect has been small.

At the same time, though, the labor market showed increasing signs of stress. Hiring has fallen sharply, the unemployment rate has crept higher and it's taking people a lot longer to find work.

The Fed last week justified its first interest-rate cut this year by pointing to a weakening labor market. By law, the Fed is supposed to make sure inflation and unemployment are low.

While inflation has taken a back seat to the labor market, Fed officials are still monitoring inflation closely.

What they especially want to see is how much goods prices continue to rise and whether a recent uptick in services costs is just a blip.

Goods are most affected by tariffs, and prices have risen since the start of the year. Goods prices only rose 0.5% in the 12 months ended in July, but they had been actually declining for most of 2023 and 2024.

Until very recently, services prices had been slowing and keeping a lid on inflation. But they perked up in June and July to raise some red flags.

Most top Fed officials believe services costs will level off again, and they'll be looking for evidence in the August PCE report.

"Friday's PCE is the next key economic data point to watch," said Rick Gardner, chief investment officer at RGA Investments in Raleigh, N.C.

-Jeffry Bartash

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-25-25 1246ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: The Fed's interest-rate cuts are fueling a stock market 'melt-up' - and ruin for some investors
data.description:
By Fabio Natalucci

A buildup in leverage would make the financial system more vulnerable and susceptible to shocks

Buyers' appetite and enthusiasm for riskier investments are rising fast.

With lax financial conditions and asset prices showing signs of froth, investors may be incentivized to take on more debt to boost expected returns.

The Federal Reserve lowered its policy rate by a quarter-point last week, noting that a shift in the balance of risks to its dual mandate of maximum employment and stable prices warranted what Chair Jerome Powell called a "risk-management cut." In addition, the "dot plot" in the Summary of Economic Projections pointed to two additional cuts by the end of the year and one more next year, for a total of 1% by the end of 2026, according to the median projection.

With conditions in the U.S. labor market cooling, the Federal Open Market Committee judged it appropriate to take a step in returning to a more neutral policy stance, even though inflation is still well above the 2% target.

But is U.S. monetary policy really tight? Most of the debate has been on the gap between measures of short-term real interest rates and estimates of a long-run neutral real rate - determined by structural forces including demographics, productivity and the global balance between savings and investment.

An alternative way to answer this question is to consider financial conditions - a summary of the overall tightness or looseness of conditions in financial markets. Ultimately, what matters are the borrowing costs of households and firms and the availability of credit. Whether monetary policy is too loose or too tight should be assessed relative to prevailing financial conditions in the broader economy.

By that standard, financial markets are ebullient. Stock prices are at or close to record highs, credit spreads are extremely compressed, volatility is low, IPO activity is accelerating and speculative markets in areas such as cryptocurrencies and meme stocks are surging.

With the Fed signaling a shift to risk-management mode and investors pricing in four cuts or even more through the end of 2026, there is a risk of a "melt-up" in financial markets in the coming months. With lax financial conditions and asset prices showing signs of froth, investors may be incentivized to take on more debt to boost expected returns from investment.

A concern is that investors will start to aggressively deploy financial leverage - juicing returns at a time when risk assets are expensive.

The economic recovery since the pandemic has been fueled in large part by government borrowing and less so by private-sector balance sheets, which look seemingly healthy in aggregate. Household debt (as a share of GDP) has continued to fall - a decline that started after the 2008 financial crisis - and growth in nonfinancial corporate debt has been muted.

A concern is that, with short-term interest rates expected to decline, investors will start to aggressively deploy financial leverage in the form of repurchase agreements, prime brokerage borrowing and financial derivatives, just to name a few, juicing returns at a time when risk assets are expensive.

Financial leverage is more insidious than traditional on-balance-sheet debt at banks. It is more difficult to assess, given that there is no simple way to aggregate different financial instruments that investors can use. It is also more difficult to track by both policymakers and market participants, given a lack of widely available, granular and timely data. Importantly, there is always a "touch point" on banks' balance sheets (Financial Stability Report - April 2025), which makes it systemic by definition.

A notable increase in the use of financial leverage would be particularly pernicious at this late stage of a long financial cycle. Retail participation in financial markets has been growing amid the gamification of trading, coinciding with a boom in speculative cryptocurrency trading. Market enthusiasm has been fueled by expectations of financial deregulation by the Trump administration, further contributing to robust risk appetite, even though tighter supervision and regulation may be warranted.

Read: Here's how stocks historically perform after Fed rate cuts when trading near record highs

Shock to stocks and bonds

A buildup in financial leverage would make the financial system more vulnerable and susceptible to shocks, especially during heightened economic and policy uncertainty.

The list of possible shocks is long: a spike in inflation as tariffs seep in; a sudden deterioration of the U.S. fiscal outlook (for example, if the Trump administration's authority on tariffs is curtailed by the Supreme Court); growing concerns about the loss of Fed independence; a disappointment of investor expectations about AI-driven productivity gains; or a deterioration of geopolitical tensions.

While each shock would play out differently in various corners of the financial system, the danger is the unraveling of a sudden, pernicious feedback loop between sharp declines in risk asset prices and forced deleveraging, which would lead to fire sales and put further downward pressure on asset prices. In a world where inflation is still above target, the tension between price stability and macro-financial stability would put the Fed in a difficult spot.

For investors who have been sitting on the sidelines, it may be tempting to cave in to FOMO - the fear of missing out - and jump in when a rising tide lifts all boats. But it is very hard to time the market. The belief that old rules of valuation, risk and financial cycles no longer apply - "this time it's different" - has been proven wrong innumerable times in the history of finance, including during episodes of rapid technological advances when enthusiasm blinded investors to the risks. This party may continue for a while, but ultimately, fundamentals will reassert themselves, and the last guests joining the crowd won't have much to celebrate.

Fabio Natalucci is the CEO of the Andersen Institute for Finance & Economics.

Plus: Fed's Powell says stocks are 'fairly highly valued.' These 3 charts show he's right.

More: Why U.S. companies could see closed markets and higher costs as globalization ends

-Fabio Natalucci

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-25-25 1250ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Here's how much money to give as a wedding gift - whether it's for a close friend, relative or distant acquaintance
data.description:
By Weston Blasi

Plus: Tips for getting creative with wedding presents if you're strapped for cash

What's an appropriate amount of money for a wedding present? Here are some tips to help decide what to give the newlyweds.

You've RSVP'd yes, booked your travel and picked out your outfit. But the last - and maybe most stressful - thing that you have to decide before attending a wedding this fall is what to give, and how much you should spend on a present to celebrate the happy couple.

How much a person should spend on a wedding gift - whether in the form of a check or something from the couple's registry - is a hotly debated topic on the internet.

Traditionally, there are some rules when it comes to wedding gifts. Some say guests should aim to cover the cost of their dinner, so that the bride and groom basically recoup the expense of inviting them. Other long-accepted guidelines include doubling your gift amount if you're bringing a plus-one and giving bigger or more expensive gifts to a family member than you would to an acquaintance.

The logic behind those rules may be flawed, however. For example, guests would only be guessing at what the cost of their meal might be, since it's unlikely the bride and groom are going to tell them. And it's not as if there's a standard price for a plate at a reception. While the average catering cost per person at a wedding is $80, according to The Knot's wedding-planning website, the numbers vary wildly based on location and other factors.

'An invitation isn't a bill.'Emily Forrest from Zola, a wedding e-commerce company

"That number will look different for everyone depending on finances, where the wedding is, and how close you are to the couple. And that's okay! An invitation isn't a bill," Emily Forrest, head of communications at Zola, a wedding e-commerce company, told MarketWatch.

There is no perfect amount to give as a wedding gift, Forrest said. Rather, people should decide based on their own financial and personal situation.

"The most important thing is showing up in the way that feels good for you," she added.

But for those desperate to avoid any wedding faux pas, here are some figures to help with the decision.

The average amount spent on a wedding gift in the U.S. this year is between $100 and $150, according to Zola. The Knot's data also show that the average guest dropped $150 on their wedding gift last year - increasing to $160 for close friends and family members and dropping slightly to $140 for more casual friends or acquaintances.

MarketWatch also polled readers to see how much they have been spending on wedding gifts this year. Most respondents fell into the $100-$200 camp, although those giving $100 or less came in a very close second.

Zola's wedding-gift guidelines, which the company calls "very loose," include these suggestions:

-- $75-$100 for acquaintances or distant relatives

-- $100-$150 for friends or relatives

-- $150-$200 or more for close friends and family - or for members of the wedding party

-- Increasing the gift amount by 50%-100% if you bring a plus-one

There are also a lot of other factors that determine how much to give, such as the cost of travel and accommodations for the festivities. According to Zola, for example, in 2025, 67% of weddings are local enough for most guests to drive to them - but that leaves about one in three weddings requiring guests to travel by air, which can get expensive.

Related: I just spent $4,400 as the best man at a wedding. It's not just bridesmaids who pay a fortune.

Zola's data show that the average cost to attend a local wedding this year is $593, but that guests spend more than double that amount - a whopping $1,314 - to attend an out-of-town wedding. That difference in travel costs means it's perfectly reasonable for guests coming from further away to spend less on a gift.

Besides travel expenses, variables like the cost of attending the wedding - including buying a new outfit and paying for activities over the wedding weekend - and how close you are to the couple may also impact how much you decide to spend on a gift, multiple wedding experts said. But etiquette experts also agree that the most important things to think about are what you're able to give, and how to give with intention.

They shared a few other tips to help wedding guests shower their soon-to-be-married friends with love - without going broke.

"Set a wedding-season budget at the start of the year if you anticipate multiple invitations," Lauren Kay, executive editor at The Knot, told MarketWatch. "Track expenses to avoid overspending on travel, outfits and gifts."

Zola's Forrest highlighted another option altogether. "If it's getting too expensive to give a gift - or attend the wedding in general - it's OK to stay home," she said. "Ultimately, it is OK to say no if an event is outside of your financial ability or comfort zone."

Some other tips from Zola and The Knot for saving money on gifts include:

-- Shopping the registry for budget-friendly options instead of giving cash

-- Pooling your money with a group of guests for a single big gift

-- Giving a personalized gift that may not have a high price tag but that can be more thoughtful

That thoughtfulness can come out in creative ways. There are Reddit threads with hundreds of comments about what users - including those going through tough financial times - have given as gifts.

There are stories about how recent job losses, or the total expense of attending a wedding, have affected the ability to give gifts, with some saying they couldn't afford to give a present at all. And in some of those cases, thoughtful friends or family members have turned to clever alternatives, like a graphic designer who worked with the bride to create save-the-date cards and wedding invitations in lieu of buying a gift.

Related: Most American weddings are a lot more extravagant than the nuptials of Amazon's Jeff Bezos

"I considered that part of her gift, although she may not know that," the Reddit user posted.

Another Reddit user, who identified themselves as a graduate student traveling a long distance for a wedding, said they created a homemade Dungeons & Dragons board game expansion for the couple.

And a commenter on MarketWatch's wedding-spending poll wrote: "I've started focusing more on thoughtful, personalized gifts rather than the price tag. In this economy, it's the meaning behind the gift that counts-sometimes a heartfelt letter and a small keepsake are more valuable than something expensive!"

The etiquette experts agree.

"At the end of the day, couples invite you to their wedding because they love you, not because of how much you spend," Kay said.

If you're just starting out on your money or career journey and have questions about how to navigate your finances, we want to hear from you. Write to Dollar Signs, MarketWatch's new advice column, at dollarsigns@marketwatch.com.

-Weston Blasi

  (END) Dow Jones Newswires

data.title: 6 top takeaways from this past earnings season favor some well-known tech and AI stocks
data.description:
By Michael Brush

Consumers will keep spending, AI and tech aren't in a bubble, and there's opportunity in Europe

Market experts say the bull has room to run.

Now that America's CEOs have finished their second-quarter earnings updates, it's time to step back and assess what they told us about where the U.S. stock market is heading.

To find out, I checked in with five market experts who, along with their analysts, manage more than $67 billion. That's a lot of brain power drilling down on SEC filings and checking in with management at companies across the economy.

Here are their high-level takeaways:

-- The bull market has room to run because the U.S. isn't about to go into a recession.

-- American consumers will continue spending.

-- Tariffs probably won't drag down the economy as much as feared.

-- AI is not in a bubble, and businesses will continue to deploy AI to boost profits.

"We didn't find a lot to not like in second-quarter earnings reports," said Nancy Tengler of Laffer Tengler Investments.

Here's more detail on the key outlooks these investment managers shared, and the stocks they expect to benefit from the economy's current growth trends:

1. No U.S. recession: As jobs numbers weaken, economists worry the U.S. is heading into a recession, which can trigger a bear market. But this isn't likely anytime soon.

One reason is that corporate results were so strong. Companies blew away estimates, including at economically-sensitive cyclical names. "The first half was in many ways remarkable," said Ed Yardeni of Yardeni Research. "Earnings growth was much better than analysts expected. The economy is doing much better than was widely feared." He cited strong capital spending and productivity growth as two more reasons to rule out recession.

Also, many companies raised earnings guidance. This is important at a time when various data gives conflicting economic reads. Said Tengler: "I don't see a recession or stagflation." Tengler is worth listening to because her "12 Best Ideas" strategy has topped the Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA by more than 10 percentage points over the past three years.

2. Growth isn't just about AI and the 'Mag Seven'; it's widespread: Bears tell us that beyond AI and the so-called Magnificent Seven Big Tech stocks, there's not much action in the economy. But the second quarter proved them wrong.

"It was encouraging to me that a number of industries contributed to strong earnings and revenue growth," said Jeremiah Buckley, manager of the Janus Henderson Balanced fund JBALX. The broad-market strength is one reason he also sees no recession near-term. Buckley is worth listening to because his fund has outperformed its Morningstar category by more than two percentage points annualized over the past three years.

Buckley cited strong results from Booking Holdings (BKNG), Delta Air Lines (DAL), Boeing (BA), Howmet Aerospace (HWM) and GE Aerospace (GE). Their results reflect healthy consumer and business spending, a positive signal for the economy. He also singled out strength at investment banks Goldman Sachs Group (GS) and Morgan Stanley (MS), and at medical technology companies Stryker (SYK) and Intuitive Surgical (ISRG).

3. There's no AI bubble: AI infrastructure-spending growth at the hyperscalers is alive and well, said Jake Seltz, who helps manage the Allspring LT Large Growth exchange-traded fund AGRW. "There is still an arms race to come up with the best products," he said.

Seltz noted that hyperscalers raised capex-spending guidance by several billion dollars each. By his estimate, Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT), Meta Platforms (META) and Amazon.com (AMZN) boosted second-quarter AI capital-spending estimates 14% to $352 billion from $310 billion in May. One of the big beneficiaries of course is Nvidia (NVDA), which Seltz said is not overvalued.

"AI spending has gotten through its DeepSeek moment," said Travis Prentice, chief investment officer at the Informed Momentum Co. DeepSeek initially appeared to provide AI at a much lower cost, but it turns out the Chinese AI company cut corners.

Prentice cited solid second-quarter results from AI arms dealers Broadcom (AVGO) and Ciena (CIEN). Other AI-related momentum stocks Prentice likes include Comfort Systems USA (FIX), Credo Technology Group Holding (CRDO) and Lumentum Holdings (LITE), which all provide parts, services and tech components for data centers.

Prentice helps manage the American Beacon IMC International Small Cap Fund TIVFX, which has outperformed its Morningstar foreign mid-cap growth category by seven percentage points over the past year.

4 . Companies continue to confirm AI is real - by using it to boost profits: Skeptics question whether companies really have any use for AI. But companies keep spending and proving them wrong. Second-quarter S&P 500 SPX operating-profit growth came in better than expected due to productivity gains produced in part with AI, Buckley said.

Here are some examples:

-- JPMorgan Chase JPM and Morgan Stanley use AI to improve fraud detection and customer services.

-- Trane Technologies TT, Eaton Corp. ETN and GE Aerospace use AI to monitor equipment to figure out when it needs servicing. These companies are growing earnings much faster than revenue, as a result.

-- Healthcare companies Amgen AMGN and Eli Lilly LLY use AI to help identify potential drugs and design trials.

-- Goldman Sachs now hires fewer associates because it can use AI to write basic financial offering documents, Tengler said. "If they can do that with fewer employees, the leverage becomes powerful," she said.

5. The U.S. consumer is OK: "Strong retail reports confirmed that consumers are alive and well. They are not retrenching," Yardeni said. This is key, because the U.S. consumer drives the economy. Prentice singled out strong business momentum at cruise lines Royal Caribbean Group (RCL) and Carnival (CCL), along with Expedia Group (EXPE) and Booking. Meanwhile, Walmart (WMT) and other retailers noted strength among wealthier consumers, but spending weakness among lower-income consumers.

6. European economies are back: "Europe has strong momentum overall, which we haven't seen for a long time," Prentice said. He cited solid results at defense and aerospace companies Rolls-Royce Holdings (RYCEY), Rheinmetall (RNMBY) and Saab (SAABY) as European nations ramp up defense spending.

Europe's banks also stand out for positive business and stock-price momentum. Here Prentice singled out Deutsche Bank (DB), National Bank of Greece (NBGIF), Alpha Bank (ALBKY), Banco Santander (SAN) and Banca Mediolanum (BNCDY).

Michael Brush is a columnist for MarketWatch. At the time of publication, he owned BKNG, GOOGL, MSFT, META, AMZN, AVGO, RCL and CCL. Brush has suggested BKNG, DAL, BA, GS, MS, GOOGL, MSFT, META, AMZN, AVGO, JPM, AMGN, LLY, RCL, CCL, EXPE and WMT in his stock newsletter, Brush Up on Stocks. Follow him on X @mbrushstocks.

More: Here's how stocks historically perform after Fed rate cuts when trading near record highs

Also read: Should you still invest in small-cap stocks after their big gains?

-Michael Brush

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires
